inputs.userGoal: Deploy a microservices-based e-commerce backend with independent catalog, order, and payment services on AKS with Helm deployments and Istio service mesh for traffic control.

architecturePrinciples: Microservices isolation — each service independently deployable and scalable
Service mesh for traffic control, mTLS, and observability
Centralized observability with Log Analytics and Application Insights
Secrets management via Key Vault with CSI driver

subnets.name: snet-aks-system
subnets.purpose: AKS system and user node pools (Azure CNI Overlay — nodes only, pods use overlay)

reasoning.whyChosen: A dedicated VNet provides network isolation for AKS workloads. /14 address space gives room for growth. Azure CNI Overlay means the subnet only needs IPs for nodes, not pods.
alternativesConsidered: kubenet (retiring March 2028)
Azure CNI (pod-per-IP, higher IP pressure)
reasoning.tradeoffs: CNI Overlay does not support VM availability sets or virtual nodes, but those are not required for this workload.

resources.name: id-ecommerce-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: User-assigned managed identity enables passwordless authentication from AKS to ACR, Key Vault, and other Azure services. Preferred over system-assigned for portability.
alternativesConsidered: System-assigned managed identity (tied to cluster lifecycle)
Service principal with secrets (less secure)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Requires explicit role assignments but provides better lifecycle management than system-assigned.

resources.dependencyReasoning: No dependencies — identity resource created first for assignment to other resources.

resources.name: log-ecommerce-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: Log Analytics is required for AKS Container Insights and serves as the backend for Application Insights. PerGB2018 is the recommended pay-as-you-go tier for new workspaces.
alternativesConsidered: CapacityReservation (better for 100+ GB/day)
Third-party monitoring (Datadog, Grafana Cloud)
reasoning.tradeoffs: PerGB2018 has variable cost; for high-volume production, CapacityReservation may be cheaper.

resources.dependencyReasoning: No dependencies — observability foundation created early.

resources.name: appi-ecommerce-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: Application Insights provides distributed tracing, request metrics, and failure analysis across all three microservices. Workspace-based mode centralizes data in Log Analytics.
alternativesConsidered: Jaeger/Zipkin on AKS (self-managed)
OpenTelemetry Collector only (no Azure-native dashboards)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Azure-native APM means vendor coupling, but provides superior integration with AKS and auto-instrumentation.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Workspace-based Application Insights requires a Log Analytics workspace for data storage.

resources.name: kv-ecommerce-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: Key Vault stores connection strings, API keys, and TLS certificates consumed by microservices via the AKS Key Vault CSI driver. RBAC authorization is the modern approach replacing access policies.
alternativesConsidered: Kubernetes Secrets (less secure, not encrypted at rest by default)
HashiCorp Vault (self-managed, higher operational overhead)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Standard tier lacks HSM-protected keys, but software keys are sufficient for connection strings and certificates.

resources.dependencyReasoning: The managed identity needs Key Vault Secrets User role assignment to access secrets via CSI driver.

resources.name: creaboreprod001

reasoning.whyChosen: Azure Container Registry stores Docker images for the catalog, order, and payment microservices. Standard SKU provides 100 GiB storage, sufficient for a three-service backend. AKS authenticates via acrPull role on the managed identity.
alternativesConsidered: Basic (10 GiB, may be tight with image history)
Premium (geo-replication, private link — overkill for single-region)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Standard lacks geo-replication and private link. Upgrade to Premium if multi-region or private networking is needed later.

resources.dependencyReasoning: AKS managed identity requires acrPull role assignment on the registry to pull images.

resources.name: aks-ecommerce-prod-001

properties.notes: Helm is used for application deployments (catalog, order, payment charts). Istio service mesh provides mTLS, traffic splitting, retries, and circuit breaking.

reasoning.whyChosen: AKS Standard tier provides 99.95% SLA with availability zones, production-grade Kubernetes. Azure CNI Overlay with Cilium gives efficient networking and network policy enforcement. Istio-based service mesh addon is fully managed by AKS, reducing operational burden. Separate system/user pools isolate infrastructure pods from application workloads.
alternativesConsidered: Azure Container Apps (simpler but less control over mesh, Helm, and networking)
Self-managed Kubernetes on VMs (full control but high operational cost)
AKS Free tier (no SLA, not suitable for production e-commerce)
reasoning.tradeoffs: AKS Standard has a per-cluster management fee (~$73/mo) but provides SLA. Istio addon adds sidecar overhead (~100MB per pod). D4s_v5 nodes provide 4 vCPU/16GB — right-size for three microservices with headroom.

resources.dependencyReasoning: AKS requires: VNet for node subnet, managed identity for authentication, Log Analytics for Container Insights, Key Vault for CSI secrets, ACR for image pulls.

resources.name: cosmos-ecommerce-prod-001

databases.name: catalog-db
databases.purpose: Product catalog — flexible schema for product attributes, categories, and variants

reasoning.whyChosen: Cosmos DB NoSQL API is ideal for the catalog service: flexible schema accommodates varying product attributes, low-latency reads for browsing, and automatic indexing. Session consistency balances performance and read-your-writes guarantees.
alternativesConsidered: PostgreSQL Flexible Server (strong consistency, relational joins, but less schema flexibility for product variants)
MongoDB Atlas (managed NoSQL but not Azure-native)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Cosmos DB is more expensive per GB than relational databases, but delivers single-digit-ms reads at global scale. Single-region deployment keeps costs manageable.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Can use VNet service endpoints or private endpoint for network isolation.

resources.name: psql-ecommerce-prod-001

databases.name: orders-db
databases.purpose: Order service — relational data with ACID transactions for order lifecycle

databases.name: payments-db
databases.purpose: Payment service — transactional records for payment processing, refunds, reconciliation

reasoning.whyChosen: PostgreSQL Flexible Server provides ACID transactions critical for order and payment services. Zone-redundant HA ensures availability. Two databases on one server keeps costs low while maintaining logical isolation. GeneralPurpose tier balances compute and cost.
alternativesConsidered: Azure SQL Database (strong option but PostgreSQL is more portable and cost-effective)
Separate servers per service (higher cost, better isolation)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Shared server means shared compute ceiling. If services have very different load patterns, separate servers may be needed later.

resources.dependencyReasoning: PostgreSQL Flexible Server supports VNet integration for private access from AKS.

resources.name: redis-ecommerce-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: Redis Cache provides low-latency caching for the catalog service (product listings, search results) and session state. Standard tier with replication ensures availability. 1 GB capacity is suitable for initial deployment.
alternativesConsidered: Premium (VNet injection, persistence — more than needed initially)
In-cluster Redis via Helm chart (self-managed, no SLA)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Standard tier lacks VNet injection and persistence. Upgrade to Premium if network isolation or data durability is needed.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Redis is accessed from AKS over public endpoint with firewall rules. No hard infrastructure dependency.

resources.name: sbns-ecommerce-prod-001

topics.name: order-events
topics.purpose: Publishes order lifecycle events (created, confirmed, shipped) consumed by payment and notification services

topics.name: payment-events
topics.purpose: Publishes payment completion/failure events consumed by order service for status updates

reasoning.whyChosen: Service Bus provides reliable async messaging between order and payment services via topics/subscriptions. This decouples services and handles transient failures with built-in retry and dead-letter queues. Standard tier supports topics (Basic does not).
alternativesConsidered: Event Grid (better for event routing, less suited for command-style messages)
RabbitMQ on AKS (self-managed, no SLA)
Premium Service Bus (dedicated capacity, VNet — overkill for initial deployment)
reasoning.tradeoffs: Standard tier uses shared infrastructure so throughput is not guaranteed under extreme load. Upgrade to Premium for dedicated messaging units.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Service Bus is a standalone messaging resource accessed by AKS pods via connection strings stored in Key Vault.

overallReasoning.summary: This architecture deploys three independent e-commerce microservices (catalog, order, payment) on AKS with Istio service mesh for traffic management, mTLS, and observability. Each service owns its data store: Cosmos DB for the flexible catalog schema, PostgreSQL for transactional order and payment data. Service Bus decouples cross-service communication. Redis caches hot catalog data. The AKS-managed Istio addon eliminates the need to self-manage the service mesh control plane. Helm charts package each microservice for independent deployment and versioning.
overallReasoning.tradeoffs: Standard-tier resources (ACR, Redis, Service Bus) keep costs reasonable but lack private networking and dedicated capacity. For production hardening, consider upgrading to Premium tiers and adding Private Endpoints. Single-region deployment has no cross-region DR — add geo-replication to Cosmos DB and PostgreSQL read replicas if HA requirements increase.

plan.validation: All resources deploy to eastus. AKS uses Azure CNI Overlay — no IP pressure from pods. Pod CIDR (internal overlay) does not overlap with VNet 10.0.0.0/14 or service CIDR 10.4.0.0/16. ACR uses managed identity (acrPull role) — no admin credentials. Key Vault uses RBAC authorization with purge protection enabled. Cosmos DB Session consistency is compatible with single-region deployment. PostgreSQL zone-redundant HA is supported on GeneralPurpose tier. Service Bus Standard supports topics. Redis Standard C1 family matches tier correctly.
